summary: I started my grade school education at Alice Robertson Elementary School in Carbondale on the west side, attending there up through the 4th grade. It was my first school experience before my family moved across the river. Those early years laid the foundation for my schooling before transitioning to other schools in Tulsa.

Carbondale, Oklahoma, was where I grew up on the west side and attended Alice Robertson Elementary School. It was my hometown during my early childhood before my family moved across the river to another part of Tulsa. That area shaped my earliest memories and schooling experiences.

After moving across the river, I attended John Marshall Elementary School for my 5th and 6th grade years. This was a new chapter after leaving the west side and Alice Robertson Elementary. It was part of my continued education in Tulsa before junior high.

For junior high, I went to Orville Wright Junior High School near 45th and Peoria. It was the next step in my education after elementary school and before high school. I had always planned to go to Edison High School after junior high, but plans changed later on.

I spent a very miserable sophomore year at Memorial High School in Tulsa because we lived south of 51st Street, and that was the school assigned to me. It wasn’t the wealthy type of school, and I didn’t have many friends there. My future wife Elaine was in the same class, but social circles were tough, and I felt out of place.

Living south of 51st Street in Tulsa meant I was assigned to Memorial High School instead of Edison, which I had originally planned to attend. That boundary line affected where I went to school and shaped my high school experience. It was a significant factor in my educational path.

After a tough year at Memorial, I transferred to Central High School in downtown Tulsa for my last two years of high school. Central was a wonderful experience with a diverse student body of all races and economic backgrounds. I loved participating in drama, opera club, and school musicals there, and it was where I discovered my singing talent with a barbershop quartet called The Highlights.
cues: last two years of high school

summary: I had originally planned to attend Edison High School after junior high, but because of where we lived, I was assigned to Memorial instead. I never actually went to Edison, although it was my intended high school. The school boundaries and policies at the time prevented me from transferring there.

Will Rogers High School was another Tulsa high school, but it wasn’t an option for me to transfer to from Memorial. Transfers were only allowed to Central High School at that time. So although I knew of Will Rogers, I never attended or transferred there.

Elaine Elslow Dodd was my future wife, and we were in the same class at Memorial High School. Even though we were classmates, social circles were tough, and I once told her she was a 'soc' because I felt out of place. She was part of my early high school experience during that difficult year.

The Highlights was the barbershop quartet I sang with during my time at Central High School. There were four of us, and we used to practice in the boys' room because of its great acoustics. Singing with The Highlights was part of my involvement in music and performing arts in high school.

I started college at John Brown University in Siloam Springs, Arkansas, in the fall of 1967, thanks to a scholarship that covered my tuition. I took broadcasting classes there, which sparked my interest in radio and television. However, after one semester, I decided to transfer because I wanted to be closer to home and experience a different environment.

Siloam Springs, Arkansas, was where John Brown University is located, and I lived there during my first semester of college. I quickly realized I had had about as much of Siloam Springs as I could take and decided to transfer back to Tulsa. It was a brief but important part of my college journey.

I had originally intended to attend the University of Oklahoma for college, but I ended up going first to John Brown University and then transferring to the University of Tulsa. OU was always an option, but the scholarship at John Brown and later programs at TU influenced my path. I never actually enrolled at OU.

I transferred to the University of Tulsa in January 1968 and completed my undergraduate degree there. TU had a strong radio and television production program, which was exactly what I wanted to pursue. I also participated in KWGS, the university's FM radio station, which was a fantastic part of my education.

In the summer of 1967, I worked at KOME, a Tulsa radio station, as an announcer thanks to a classmate's brother who was an ad salesman there. KOME had been a big station decades before but was somewhat downscale by then. I enjoyed working there, handling live station breaks and playing network shows like Arthur Godfrey and Don McNeill's Breakfast Club.

CBS was one of the networks whose programming we aired at KOME during my time there in 1967. We played shows like the Arthur Godfrey Show, which was part of the live network content I was exposed to as a young announcer. It was a key part of the radio experience back then.

ABC was another network whose programs we broadcast on KOME, including Don McNeill's Breakfast Club from Chicago. This exposure to national radio shows was part of my early broadcasting experience in 1967. It helped me understand the broader media landscape beyond local radio.

Arthur Godfrey was a host of a popular radio show that we aired on KOME during my time there in 1967. His show was part of the CBS network programming that I helped broadcast as a young announcer. It was a memorable part of the radio content I worked with early in my career.

Don McNeill was the host of the Breakfast Club, a radio show from Chicago that we aired on KOME in 1967. This ABC program was part of the national network content I was involved with as a radio announcer. It gave me a taste of the professional broadcasting world early on.

Chicago was the city from which Don McNeill's Breakfast Club was broadcast, a show I aired on KOME during the summer of 1967. Although I never lived there, Chicago was part of the national radio network programming that influenced my early broadcasting experience. It represented the broader reach of radio beyond Tulsa.

Robert Welch was the man behind the Conservative Viewpoint program that we aired in 15-minute segments on KOME. I didn’t realize at the time that he was the head of the John Birch Society, something I learned decades later. His program was part of the conservative content on the station during my early radio days.

I only learned years later that Robert Welch, whose Conservative Viewpoint program I aired on KOME, was the head of the John Birch Society. At the time, I was just focused on my broadcasting duties and didn’t know much about the organization. It was an interesting revelation about the political content on the station.

KWGS was the University of Tulsa’s FM radio station where I participated as part of my broadcast studies. Founded in 1948 by William Grove Skelly of Skelly Oil, it was the first FM station in Tulsa and played classical music, including the Texaco Metropolitan Opera. My professor Ed Dumit taught us there, and it was a vital part of my education and training in broadcasting.

William Grove Skelly was the benefactor of KWGS, the University of Tulsa’s FM radio station. He was associated with Skelly Oil and helped establish the station in 1948, the year before I was born. His support made it possible for students like me to gain hands-on broadcasting experience at TU.

Skelly Oil was the company connected to William Grove Skelly, who funded KWGS, the University of Tulsa’s FM station. This connection helped create a unique learning environment for broadcasting students like me. It was part of the legacy that supported my education in radio and television production.

Ed Dumit was my broadcast studies professor at the University of Tulsa and a truly magnificent teacher. He co-wrote the textbook on broadcast announcing and made us learn to pronounce every classical piece, composer, and orchestra for our work on KWGS. I still miss him to this day, as he was a major influence on my broadcasting career.

Ben Henneke co-wrote the broadcast announcing textbook with my professor Ed Dumit and later became president of the University of Tulsa. His work helped shape the curriculum I studied in broadcasting. Though I didn’t know him personally, his contributions were part of my education.

The Texaco Metropolitan Opera was a live broadcast program we ran on KWGS every Saturday while I was a student at the University of Tulsa. The station played nothing but classical music, and this opera broadcast was a key part of our programming. It was a challenging and rewarding experience to be involved in such a prestigious show.
